summary:
The Third Buddhist Council (c. 250 BC) was convened by [[event:1098|Ashoka the Great]] at Pataliputra (Patna), which standardized the Theravada Buddhist canon (the Pali Tipitaka) and authorized the missionary expeditions that spread Buddhism across Asia, including the mission of [[event:1101|Spread of Buddhism under Ashoka]]'s Mahinda to Sri Lanka.

Buddhist tradition records two earlier councils: the First (at Rajagriha, shortly after the Buddha's death) established the oral canon, and the Second (at Vesali, about 100 years later) addressed disciplinary controversies. The Third Council, presided over by the monk Moggaliputta Tissa, aimed to purify the sangha (monastic community) of heterodox views that had proliferated during Ashoka's generous patronage — as the patronage attracted monks more interested in benefits than in genuine practice. The council reaffirmed Theravada orthodoxy and compiled the Kathavatthu (Points of Controversy), a text distinguishing accepted Theravada positions from heterodox views. The council then dispatched missionaries in all directions: to Sri Lanka (Mahinda, Ashoka's son), to the Himalayan regions, to Burma/Myanmar, to Central Asia, and to the Greek kingdoms in the west — making it arguably the most consequential event in Buddhist missionary history.
